title: Employment Rights Act 2025
desc: The most significant reform of UK employment rights in a generation. Royal Assent 18 December 2025. Removes the 2-year qualifying period for unfair dismissal, introduces rights for zero hours workers, reforms SSP, strengthens trade union rights, and extends protections during redundancy and family leave. The majority of provisions are NOT YET IN FORCE — commencement dates to be set by secondary legislation.

parts.title: Unfair Dismissal — Qualifying Period and Probationary Period

sections.title: Removal of qualifying period for unfair dismissal
sections.summary: Removes the 2-year qualifying period in ERA 1996 s.108. All employees will have the right not to be unfairly dismissed from their first day of employment.
sections.text:
(1) Section 108 of the Employment Rights Act 1996 (qualifying period of employment) is amended as follows.

(2) In subsection (1), the Secretary of State may by regulations provide that the period prescribed is to be zero, with the effect that section 94 of the 1996 Act (the right not to be unfairly dismissed) applies from the first day of employment.

(4) Where the period prescribed is zero, the Secretary of State may by regulations make provision about a procedure to be followed by an employer when dismissing an employee during an initial period of employment (the “statutory initial employment period”), and consequences for the employer and employee where that procedure is not followed.
sections.eraNote: Commencement: Not yet in force. Commencement date to be set by regulations. Government consultation on probationary period procedure ongoing as at March 2026. Anticipated commencement: late 2026 or 2027. Once commenced, s.108 ERA 1996 will be amended so that no qualifying period applies. A statutory probationary period procedure is expected to be introduced — employers who follow the procedure when dismissing during the initial period will have additional protection. ACEI impact: HIGH — affects every employer who dismisses within the first 2 years of employment.
refs: s.108 ERA 1996 (Qualifying Period — to be amended)
s.94 ERA 1996 (Right Not to Be Unfairly Dismissed)

sections.title: Statutory initial employment period
sections.summary: Secretary of State may by regulations establish a statutory initial employment period (maximum 9 months) during which a lighter-touch dismissal procedure applies.
sections.text:
(1) The Secretary of State may by regulations make provision for a “statutory initial employment period” for the purposes of Part X of the 1996 Act.

(2) Regulations under this section may, in particular, provide for—
(a) the length of the statutory initial employment period (which must not exceed 9 months),
(b) the procedure to be followed by an employer who dismisses an employee during the statutory initial employment period,
(c) the effect on unfair dismissal claims where the employer has or has not followed that procedure.

(3) In making regulations, the Secretary of State must have regard to the desirability of ensuring that employees are protected against unfair dismissal from the start of their employment, whilst giving employers an appropriate opportunity to assess whether a new employee is suitable for the role.
sections.eraNote: Commencement: Not yet in force. The Government has indicated the probationary period will be 9 months. Consultation response expected mid-2026. The procedure is anticipated to require employers to: hold a meeting before dismissal, give written reasons, allow the employee to be accompanied, and provide a right of appeal.
refs: ACAS Code on Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures

sections.title: Written reasons for dismissal — Day 1 right
sections.summary: Removes the requirement for 2 years' service before an employee can request written reasons for dismissal. All employees entitled from Day 1.
sections.text:
(1) Section 92 of the Employment Rights Act 1996 is amended to omit subsection (2) (which requires two years' continuous employment before an employee can request written reasons for dismissal).

(2) After subsection (1) insert—
"(1A) An employer must, whether or not a request has been made under subsection (1), provide an employee with a written statement of reasons for dismissal where the employee is dismissed during the statutory initial employment period (within the meaning given by section 2 of the Employment Rights Act 2025)."

(3) An employer must comply with a request under section 92(1) within 14 days beginning with the day on which the request is made.
sections.eraNote: Commencement: Not yet in force. Currently only employees with 2+ years service (or pregnant/maternity leave employees) can request written reasons. On commencement, all employees will be entitled. ACEI impact: MEDIUM — employers should document dismissal reasons thoroughly from Day 1.
refs: s.92 ERA 1996 (Written Statement of Reasons — to be amended)

parts.title: Zero Hours Contracts and Agency Workers

sections.title: Right to guaranteed hours
sections.summary: Qualifying workers on zero hours or variable hours contracts may be entitled to a contract reflecting their average hours over a reference period.
sections.text:
(1) A qualifying worker who, during a reference period, works hours for their employer that are not guaranteed under their contract is entitled to be offered a contract that guarantees those hours (a “guaranteed hours contract”).

(2) For the purposes of this section—
(a) a “reference period” is such period as may be prescribed by the Secretary of State,
(b) a worker is a “qualifying worker” if they satisfy such conditions as may be prescribed.

(3) The employer must make the offer of a guaranteed hours contract within the period prescribed by the Secretary of State beginning after the end of the reference period.

(4) The terms of the guaranteed hours contract must guarantee the worker a number of hours equal to the average hours worked per week during the reference period, and reflect the days and times the worker has regularly worked during the reference period.
sections.eraNote: Commencement: Not yet in force. Secondary legislation required. Expected commencement: 2026–2027. This is the most operationally complex ERA 2025 provision — employers with large zero hours workforces will need to review all zero hours contracts and implement tracking systems. ACEI impact: VERY HIGH for hospitality, retail, healthcare, and logistics sectors.
refs: s.9 ERA 2025 (Right to Payment for Cancelled Shifts)

sections.title: Right to reasonable notice of shifts
sections.summary: Workers are entitled to reasonable notice of when they are required to work. Notice period to be prescribed by regulations.
sections.text:
(1) A worker who does not have guaranteed working hours is entitled to be given reasonable notice by their employer of—
(a) the fact that they are required to work, and
(b) the hours they are required to work.

(2) The minimum period of notice to which a worker is entitled under this section is such period as may be prescribed by the Secretary of State.

(3) An employer must give notice under this section in writing.

(4) If an employer fails to give notice as required by this section, the worker is entitled to decline to work during the period in respect of which notice was not given, and to present a complaint to an employment tribunal.
sections.eraNote: Commencement: Not yet in force. The Government is considering a minimum notice period of 1–2 weeks. Regulations will prescribe the precise period. ACEI impact: HIGH for sectors where last-minute rota changes are common.

sections.title: Right to payment for cancelled, moved or curtailed shifts
sections.summary: Workers who have shifts cancelled, moved, or curtailed at short notice are entitled to payment reflecting the lost time.
sections.text:
(1) Where an employer cancels, moves or curtails a shift—
(a) after the worker has received notice of the shift under section 8, and
(b) within such period before the shift as may be prescribed,
the worker is entitled to a payment from the employer in respect of the shift or the curtailed portion of it.

(2) The amount of the payment to which a worker is entitled is such amount as may be prescribed.

(3) This section does not apply if the cancellation, movement or curtailment is caused by circumstances genuinely outside the employer's control, provided the employer took all reasonable steps to avoid it.
sections.eraNote: Commencement: Not yet in force. Regulations required. Expected to apply when shifts are cancelled within 1–2 weeks of the shift date. The “genuinely outside employer's control” exception is anticipated to be construed narrowly.

sections.title: Automatic unfair dismissal — zero hours
sections.summary: Dismissal for exercising or seeking to exercise rights under zero hours provisions of ERA 2025 is automatically unfair regardless of length of service.
sections.text:
(1) An employee who is dismissed is to be regarded as unfairly dismissed if the reason (or, if more than one, the principal reason) for the dismissal is that the employee exercised, or proposed to exercise, the right to a guaranteed hours contract under section 7, the right to reasonable notice under section 8, or the right to payment for a cancelled, moved or curtailed shift under section 9.

(2) Section 108(1) of the 1996 Act (qualifying period of employment) does not apply in relation to a dismissal falling within this section.
refs: s.108 ERA 1996 (Qualifying Period — does not apply)

sections.title: Removal of waiting days for SSP
sections.summary: Removes the 3 waiting days before Statutory Sick Pay becomes payable. SSP will be payable from the first day of incapacity.
sections.text:
(1) Section 155 of the Social Security Contributions and Benefits Act 1992 (days for which statutory sick pay is payable) is amended to omit the words providing that SSP is not payable for the first three days of incapacity (the “waiting days”).

(2) Accordingly, statutory sick pay is payable from the first qualifying day of the period of incapacity for work.
sections.eraNote: Commencement: Expected October 2026 — one of the earlier ERA 2025 provisions anticipated to come into force. Currently SSP is only payable from the 4th day of sickness. On commencement, employers will be liable for SSP from Day 1. Employers who currently pay contractual sick pay from Day 1 will see no change. Those who only pay statutory minimums will face increased cost. ACEI impact: MEDIUM — increases SSP liability for employers with high short-term sickness absence.
refs: SSCBA 1992 s.155 (Waiting Days — to be amended)
DWP Statutory Sick Pay: Employer Guidance

sections.title: Lower earnings limit for SSP
sections.summary: SSP will be payable to employees earning below the lower earnings limit, at a rate proportional to their earnings.
sections.text:
(1) Section 157 of the Social Security Contributions and Benefits Act 1992 is amended to provide that employees earning below the lower earnings limit may be entitled to statutory sick pay at a reduced rate.

(2) Where an employee's average weekly earnings are below the lower earnings limit, the rate of statutory sick pay payable to that employee is such percentage of their average weekly earnings as may be prescribed by the Secretary of State.
sections.eraNote: Commencement: Not yet in force. Regulations required to prescribe the reduced rate. This provision extends SSP entitlement to lower-paid workers who currently do not qualify because they earn below the lower earnings limit (£123/week 2024–25). Estimated to bring 1.3 million additional workers into SSP entitlement.
refs: SSCBA 1992 s.157 (Lower Earnings Limit — to be amended)

sections.title: Paternity leave — removal of qualifying period
sections.summary: Removes the 26-week qualifying period for statutory paternity leave. Day 1 right for all employees.
sections.text:
(1) The Paternity and Adoption Leave Regulations 2002 are amended to omit the requirement that the employee must have been continuously employed for a period of not less than 26 weeks ending with the week immediately preceding the 14th week before the expected week of childbirth.

(2) The effect of the amendment is that an employee is entitled to paternity leave from the first day of their employment.
sections.eraNote: Commencement: Not yet in force. Currently paternity leave requires 26 weeks' service. ACEI impact: LOW-MEDIUM — increases paternity leave entitlement for new employees in their first 26 weeks.

sections.title: Paternity leave — timing
sections.summary: Paternity leave may be taken at any point within 52 weeks of birth or adoption, rather than in the first 56 days.
sections.text:
(1) The Paternity and Adoption Leave Regulations 2002 are amended to provide that paternity leave may be taken at any time within the period of 52 weeks beginning with the birth of the child or the day of placement for adoption.

(2) An employee must give their employer notice of their intention to take paternity leave at least 28 days before the first day of their intended leave period.

(3) Paternity leave under the amended regulations may be taken in two separate one-week blocks (or one two-week block), not necessarily consecutively.
sections.eraNote: Commencement: Not yet in force. Currently paternity leave must be taken within 56 days of birth or adoption. The extended 52-week window and ability to split leave will allow fathers to take leave when it is most needed. ACEI impact: LOW — increases flexibility but does not extend total entitlement.

sections.title: Bereavement leave — extension
sections.summary: Extends the right to bereavement leave beyond the loss of a child under 18 to cover other close family members.
sections.text:
(1) The Parental Bereavement (Leave and Pay) Act 2018 is amended to extend the right to statutory bereavement leave to employees who suffer the death of—
(a) a spouse or civil partner,
(b) a co-habiting partner,
(c) a parent or step-parent,
(d) a sibling or step-sibling,
(e) a grandparent,
(f) a miscarriage at any stage of pregnancy.

(2) An employee is entitled to two weeks' bereavement leave in respect of each bereavement specified in subsection (1).

(3) Bereavement leave under this section is a Day 1 right — no qualifying period applies.
sections.eraNote: Commencement: Not yet in force. Currently the Parental Bereavement (Leave and Pay) Act 2018 only covers the loss of a child under 18. This provision significantly extends coverage. ACEI impact: MEDIUM — employers will need to update bereavement leave policies and train managers.

sections.title: Redundancy during pregnancy and family leave — enhanced protection
sections.summary: Extends the protected period against redundancy selection during and after maternity, adoption, and shared parental leave.
sections.text:
(1) Employees are protected from redundancy selection during—
(a) any period of ordinary or additional maternity leave,
(b) any period of adoption leave,
(c) any period of shared parental leave of two weeks or more, and
(d) a period of 18 months from the date of the child's birth or adoption, regardless of whether the employee has returned to work.

(2) During a protected period, an employee under notice of redundancy must be offered any suitable available vacancy in preference to other redundant employees.
sections.eraNote: Commencement: Not yet in force. Consolidates and extends protections into primary legislation. ACEI impact: HIGH — redundancy exercises involving pregnant women or employees on family leave require very careful management.

sections.title: Industrial action — removal of 12-week protection limit
sections.summary: Removes the 12-week limit on automatic unfair dismissal protection for employees taking protected industrial action.
sections.text:
(1) Section 238A of the Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992 is amended to omit subsection (3), which provides that the protection against automatic unfair dismissal for protected industrial action ceases to apply after 12 weeks of industrial action.

(2) The effect is that employees taking protected industrial action will be protected against dismissal for the full duration of the industrial action, provided the action remains protected.
sections.eraNote: Commencement: Not yet in force. ACEI impact: HIGH for employers with trade union recognition — significantly strengthens workers' position in industrial disputes.
refs: TULRCA 1992 s.237 (Employee Taking Part in Industrial Action)

sections.title: Access to workplaces — trade union officials
sections.summary: Trade union officials have a new right of access to workplaces to meet workers, recruit members, and carry out union activities.
sections.text:
(1) A trade union official has the right to access to the employer's premises for the purpose of—
(a) meeting workers who are members of the union,
(b) recruiting workers to membership of the union,
(c) carrying out trade union activities in relation to workers at the premises.

(2) The right of access under this section is subject to—
(a) the official giving the employer reasonable notice (not less than 48 hours, save in urgent circumstances),
(b) the access taking place at a reasonable time and in a reasonable manner,
(c) compliance with reasonable requirements of the employer relating to health and safety or security.

(3) An employer may not impose conditions on the right of access that have the effect of preventing or significantly limiting the exercise of the right.
sections.eraNote: Commencement: Not yet in force. This is a new right — there is currently no general right of access for trade union officials to non-union workplaces. ACEI impact: MEDIUM-HIGH for employers without union recognition.
refs: TULRCA 1992 ss.168–172 (Time Off for Trade Union Duties)

sections.title: Equality action plans
sections.summary: Large employers (250+ employees) must produce and publish equality action plans covering gender, ethnicity, and disability pay gaps.
sections.text:
(1) An employer who has 250 or more employees must produce an equality action plan and publish it on a publicly accessible website.

(2) An equality action plan must set out the employer's—
(a) current position on gender pay, ethnicity pay, and disability pay gaps,
(b) targets for reducing those gaps over a three-year period,
(c) specific actions the employer intends to take to achieve those targets,
(d) progress against any targets set in previous equality action plans.

(3) The plan must be reviewed and updated annually.
sections.eraNote: Commencement: Not yet in force. Regulations required. Extends existing gender pay gap reporting to include an action plan and extends to ethnicity and disability pay gaps. ACEI impact: MEDIUM for large employers.
refs: Equality Act 2010 s.78 (Regulations as to Disclosure)

sections.title: Third party harassment — employer liability reinstated
sections.summary: Reinstates employer liability for third party harassment of employees, which was removed in 2013.
sections.text:
(1) Section 40 of the Equality Act 2010 is amended to provide that an employer (A) is liable for harassment of an employee (B) by a third party if—
(a) B is harassed in the course of their employment by a third party,
(b) A knew that B had been harassed in the course of their employment by a third party on at least two previous occasions, and
(c) A failed to take such steps as would have been reasonably practicable to prevent the third party from doing so.
sections.eraNote: Commencement: Not yet in force. Third-party harassment liability was removed from the Equality Act 2010 by the Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013. ERA 2025 reinstates a similar (though modified) duty. ACEI impact: HIGH for employers in customer-facing industries (hospitality, retail, healthcare, transport).
refs: EHRC Employer Liability for Third Party Harassment Guidance

sections.title: Pay secrecy clauses — prohibited
sections.summary: Pay secrecy clauses that prevent employees discussing their pay with colleagues are rendered unenforceable.
sections.text:
(1) A term of a worker's contract that purports to prevent or restrict the worker from—
(a) disclosing their remuneration to any person, or
(b) seeking to obtain information about the remuneration of another person,
has no effect in relation to a disclosure or attempt made for the purpose of establishing whether there is a connection between remuneration and a relevant protected characteristic.

(2) A worker who makes a disclosure or seeks information as described in subsection (1) has the right not to be subjected to any detriment as a result.
sections.eraNote: Commencement: Not yet in force. ACEI impact: MEDIUM — employers should review standard contract terms that include pay secrecy clauses.
refs: Equality Act 2010 s.77 (Pay Secrecy — existing provision)

sections.title: Dismissal and re-engagement — automatic unfair dismissal
sections.summary: Dismissal and re-engagement (fire and rehire) to impose new terms and conditions is automatically unfair in most circumstances.
sections.text:
(1) An employee who is dismissed is to be regarded as unfairly dismissed if the reason (or principal reason) for the dismissal is that—
(a) the employee refused to agree to a variation of their contract of employment, or
(b) the employer wished to employ another person (or the employee themselves) under a contract with different terms and conditions.

(2) Subsection (1) does not apply if the employer can show that—
(a) the variation was necessary to prevent serious financial difficulties that threatened the viability of the business,
(b) the employer had fully consulted with the employee and their representatives before the dismissal,
(c) the new terms were as favourable to the employee as the employer could reasonably offer in the circumstances, and
(d) the circumstances were such that a reasonable employer could have concluded that dismissal was necessary.

(3) The right under this section applies regardless of the employee's length of service.
sections.eraNote: Commencement: Not yet in force. The ACAS Code on Dismissal and Re-engagement (2024) already discourages the practice but ERA 2025 creates statutory protection. ACEI impact: VERY HIGH — employers should review any planned restructuring involving changes to terms and conditions. The narrow exception requires genuine financial necessity, full consultation, and as-favourable-as-possible terms.
refs: ACAS Code of Practice on Dismissal and Re-engagement 2024
Tesco Stores Ltd v USDAW [2022] (Fire and Rehire in Courts)

sections.title: Single enforcement body — Fair Work Agency
sections.summary: Establishes the Fair Work Agency to enforce employment rights, bringing together HMRC NMW enforcement, GLAA, and Employment Agency Standards Inspectorate.
sections.eraNote: Commencement: Not yet in force. The Fair Work Agency's power to bring tribunal claims on behalf of workers is particularly significant — it means workers who would not otherwise pursue claims may have them brought on their behalf. ACEI impact: HIGH — increases risk of enforcement action for NMW, working time, and holiday pay non-compliance.

sections.title: Commencement
sections.summary: Provisions come into force on such day as the Secretary of State may by regulations appoint. Different provisions may be commenced on different days.
sections.text:
(1) The following provisions come into force on the day this Act is passed (18 December 2025)—
(a) this section,
(b) section 146 (power to make regulations),
(c) section 147 (extent),
(d) section 148 (short title).

(2) All other provisions of this Act come into force on such day as the Secretary of State may by regulations appoint.

(3) Regulations under subsection (2) may appoint different days for different purposes, and make transitional or saving provisions.
sections.eraNote: As at March 2026, no commencement regulations have been made for the substantive provisions. Government indicative timeline: SSP (s.21) — October 2026; Paternity Leave (ss.26–27) — 2026; Zero Hours (ss.7–13) — 2026–2027; Unfair Dismissal qualifying period (s.1) — 2026–2027; Trade Union provisions (ss.49–89) — 2026–2027; Fire and Rehire (s.131) — under review. All dates subject to change.
refs: GOV.UK Employment Rights Bill: Implementation Roadmap

parliamentaryOrigin.sponsoringMinister: Angela Rayner MP (Deputy Prime Minister and Secretary of State for Levelling Up, Housing and Communities; Minister for Women and Equality with special responsibility for employment rights)

readings.keyPoints: Employment Rights Bill introduced, fulfilling the Labour manifesto pledge to legislate within the first 100 days of government. The Bill contained 28 individual employment law reforms across 151 sections and multiple schedules — the most comprehensive single piece of employment legislation since the Employment Relations Act 1999.
readings.significance: Delivered within the first 100 days as promised. The scale of the Bill was unprecedented — covering unfair dismissal, zero hours contracts, SSP, family leave, trade union rights, fire and rehire, harassment, and the creation of the Fair Work Agency.

readings.keyPoints: Angela Rayner opened the debate. The Bill was presented as delivering the government's 'Plan to Make Work Pay' and the 'New Deal for Working People' manifesto commitment. Key provisions debated: Day 1 unfair dismissal rights, zero hours contract reforms, fire and rehire restrictions, SSP reforms, trade union access rights, and the Fair Work Agency. The Opposition criticised the Bill's reliance on delegated legislation and raised concerns about the impact on business confidence and recruitment. The Bill passed Second Reading.
readings.significance: The Second Reading debate established the policy architecture. Rayner's statements on the purpose of each provision are admissible under Pepper v Hart.

readings.hansardRef: HC Public Bill Committee, Employment Rights Bill, Session 2024-25
readings.keyPoints: Extensive Committee proceedings. Oral evidence sessions heard from TUC, CBI, CIPD, Federation of Small Businesses, ACAS, Resolution Foundation, and others. Over 400 amendments debated. Key contested provisions: zero hours contract reference periods, the statutory probationary period length, fire and rehire exceptions, trade union access rights, and the delegation of detail to secondary legislation. The Delegated Powers and Regulatory Reform Committee criticised several powers as 'inappropriately broad'.
readings.significance: The Committee stage revealed the operational complexity of the zero hours provisions and the tension between the government's ambition and the need for flexibility in implementation through secondary legislation.

readings.keyPoints: Two-day Report Stage. Government amendments on trade union recognition (new cl.57/Sch.6), simplified ballot requirements, and electronic balloting. The House divided on government amendments. The Opposition described the Bill as 'a bad bill' despite containing 'many good and well-intentioned measures', arguing the government had 'failed to get the balance right between employees and employers'.
readings.significance: Report Stage produced significant government amendments, particularly on trade union recognition and industrial action provisions.

readings.keyPoints: Extensive Lords scrutiny. Second Reading: April 2025. Committee: May-July 2025. Report: autumn 2025. Key Lords debates on: the unfair dismissal compensation cap (Lords proposed removal, government initially resisted), the probationary period framework, zero hours contract definitions, fire and rehire financial distress exception, and trade union access rights. The Constitution Committee's 7th Report criticised the Bill's excessive use of delegated powers. The Lords secured the abandonment of Day 1 unfair dismissal in favour of a 6-month qualifying period. The Lords also pressed for a review of the compensation cap before its removal could be commenced.
readings.significance: The Lords fundamentally shaped the final Act. The retreat from Day 1 unfair dismissal to 6 months, and the requirement for a review before compensation cap removal, were the two most significant policy shifts during the Bill's passage.

readings.keyPoints: Parliamentary ping-pong focused on the unfair dismissal compensation cap. The Lords proposed retaining the cap pending a review. The Commons rejected the Lords' position. The Lords ultimately acquiesced on 16 December 2025, accepting the removal provision subject to a review or impact assessment before commencement.
readings.significance: The ping-pong resolved the final contested issue. The compensation cap removal is enacted but subject to a review before commencement — meaning it may never be commenced or may be commenced in modified form.

readings.keyPoints: Employment Rights Act 2025 (c.36) received Royal Assent on 18 December 2025. Only procedural provisions (commencement, extent, short title) came into force immediately. Substantive provisions require commencement orders. Some industrial action provisions came into force 2 months after Royal Assent (18 February 2026). The government indicated over 25 consultations and 80+ sets of regulations would follow.
readings.significance: The most significant single piece of employment legislation in the UK since the Employment Relations Act 1999. The phased commencement model — with over 25 consultations and 80+ regulation-making powers — means the Act will be implemented over 2026-2028. For employers, the Act signals a structural shift in the employment relationship framework.

significantAmendments.text: Originally proposed Day 1 unfair dismissal rights. Retreated to 6-month qualifying period during Lords stages.
significantAmendments.outcome: Enacted as s.1 — Secretary of State may prescribe qualifying period as zero. Statutory initial employment period (max 9 months) provided for.
significantAmendments.significance: The single most significant policy shift during the Bill's passage. The retreat from Day 1 to 6 months reflected employer concerns about the impact on recruitment and the need for a probationary framework.

significantAmendments.text: The reference period, qualifying conditions, and detailed operation of the guaranteed hours right were left to secondary legislation.
significantAmendments.outcome: Enacted as s.7. The Delegated Powers Committee criticised the breadth of the regulation-making power.
significantAmendments.significance: The most operationally complex provision in the Act. The government acknowledged that the detail would be determined through consultation and secondary legislation.

significantAmendments.text: The original Bill made fire and rehire automatically unfair. The exception for financial distress was debated extensively.
significantAmendments.outcome: Enacted as s.131 with a narrowly drawn exception requiring genuine financial necessity, full consultation, and as-favourable-as-possible terms.
significantAmendments.significance: The fire and rehire provisions were described as 'softened' relative to earlier proposals but still represent a fundamental restriction on employer restructuring powers.

keyDebates.ministerialStatement: Angela Rayner presented the removal of the 2-year qualifying period as the centrepiece of the Bill, arguing that employees should not have to work for two years before being protected from unfair dismissal. The government subsequently retreated to a 6-month qualifying period during Lords stages.
keyDebates.significance: The policy shift from Day 1 to 6 months was the defining compromise of the Bill's passage. The original Pepper v Hart statement at Second Reading must be read alongside the subsequent amendment.

keyDebates.ministerialStatement: The government confirmed that fire and rehire would be automatically unfair except in narrow circumstances of genuine financial distress threatening the viability of the business. The exception was drawn from the P&O Ferries incident (2022) where the government committed to legislating against the practice.
keyDebates.significance: The P&O Ferries mass dismissal (March 2022) was the catalytic event for the fire and rehire provisions. The ministerial statements at Report Stage define the narrow scope of the financial distress exception.

precedingDocuments.title: Labour's Plan to Make Work Pay: Delivering a New Deal for Working People
precedingDocuments.significance: The governing policy framework for the Bill. Set out the 28 reforms subsequently enacted.

precedingDocuments.title: Employment Rights Green Paper: A New Deal for Working People
precedingDocuments.significance: The original policy proposal, updated in 2024 as 'Plan to Make Work Pay'.

precedingDocuments.title: GOV.UK Employment Rights Bill: Next Steps to Make Work Pay
precedingDocuments.significance: Published alongside the Bill on 10 October 2024. Set out the government's implementation approach and confirmed that most provisions would be commenced through secondary legislation following consultation.

precedingDocuments.title: GOV.UK Employment Rights Bill: Implementation Roadmap
precedingDocuments.significance: Set out the phased commencement timetable. Confirmed over 25 consultations and indicated commencement dates ranging from April 2026 to 2028.

precedingDocuments.title: P&O Ferries Mass Dismissal — Government Response
precedingDocuments.publisher: Department for Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy
precedingDocuments.significance: The P&O Ferries incident (March 2022) where 800 workers were dismissed without consultation catalysed political support for fire and rehire reform. Both Conservative and Labour governments committed to legislating in response.

explanatoryNotes.note: Comprehensive Explanatory Notes accompany the Bill. The Notes were updated at each parliamentary stage (as introduced, as amended in Committee, as amended on Report, as brought from the Commons, as amended in Lords Committee/Report). The Explanatory Notes are the primary reference for the policy intent of each provision.

selectCommitteeReports.title: 7th Report: Employment Rights Bill (2024-25)
selectCommitteeReports.significance: Criticised the Bill's excessive use of delegated powers, noting that key policy decisions were left to secondary legislation rather than being set out in the Bill itself.

selectCommitteeReports.committee: Delegated Powers and Regulatory Reform Committee (House of Lords)
selectCommitteeReports.title: 20th Report: Employment Rights Bill
selectCommitteeReports.significance: Found several regulation-making powers 'inappropriately broad', particularly the zero hours contract exemption power in new s.27BD(6). Recommended amendments to constrain delegated powers.

inForce.provision: Repeal of Strikes (Minimum Service Levels) Act 2023
s.77 (ERA 2025) — Removal of 12-week protected period for industrial action dismissal (TULRCA s.238A)
Trade union political fund ballot exemption

expectedApril2026: Doubling of protective award for collective redundancy consultation failure (s.189 TULRCA — 90 to 180 days)
Whistleblower protection strengthening for sexual harassment disclosures
expectedOctober2026: Duty to inform workers of right to join a trade union
expected2027: Unfair dismissal qualifying period change (s.1) — 6 months
'All reasonable steps' preventive duty for sexual harassment
Equality action plans for large employers (s.90)
subjectToReview: Removal of unfair dismissal compensation cap — subject to impact assessment/review before commencement

leadingCases.name: Kostal UK Ltd v Dunkley [2021] UKSC 47
leadingCases.heldText: Direct pay offers to workers during collective bargaining breached TULRCA s.145B. The ERA 2025 trade union provisions further strengthen the protections considered in this case.
leadingCases.significance: The ERA 2025 trade union access and recognition reforms build on the framework considered by the Supreme Court.

leadingCases.name: Uber BV v Aslam [2021] UKSC 5
leadingCases.heldText: Uber drivers were workers. The ERA 2025 zero hours and guaranteed hours provisions address the same gig economy workforce the Supreme Court considered.
leadingCases.significance: The ERA 2025 zero hours provisions are a legislative response to the same structural labour market issues.

leadingCases.name: Tesco Stores Ltd v USDAW [2022] UKSC 25
leadingCases.heldText: The Supreme Court held that Tesco could not be injuncted from dismissing and re-engaging workers on less favourable terms, even where the contract contained 'retained pay' provisions. ERA 2025 s.131 directly responds to this by making fire and rehire automatically unfair.
leadingCases.significance: The fire and rehire provisions in ERA 2025 s.131 are a direct legislative response to the legal position established in this case and the P&O Ferries incident.

leadingCases.name: USDAW v Ethel Austin Ltd [2015] ICR 675
leadingCases.heldText: 'Establishment' in TULRCA s.188(1) means the local employment unit. ERA 2025 proposes to override this interpretation by removing the 'at one establishment' limitation.
leadingCases.significance: ERA 2025 directly overrides this Court of Appeal interpretation.

leadingCases.name: Unite the Union v Nailard [2018] EWCA Civ 1203
leadingCases.heldText: Following the ERRA 2013 repeal of EqA 2010 s.40(2)-(4), the remaining EqA provisions cannot create employer liability for third-party harassment. ERA 2025 s.94 reinstates third-party harassment liability.
leadingCases.significance: ERA 2025 s.94 directly addresses the gap identified by the Court of Appeal.

leadingCases.name: Mercer v Alternative Future Group [2022] EWCA Civ 379
leadingCases.heldText: ERA 1996 s.148(1) was incompatible with ECHR Article 11. ERA 2025 strengthens industrial action protections in response.
leadingCases.significance: ERA 2025 industrial action provisions respond to the ECHR incompatibility identified by the Court of Appeal.

leadingCases.name: Royal Mencap Society v Tomlinson-Blake [2021] UKSC 6
leadingCases.heldText: Sleep-in care workers are not 'working' for NMW purposes during sleep periods. The ERA 2025 Fair Work Agency provisions strengthen NMW enforcement across the care sector.
leadingCases.significance: The Fair Work Agency's enhanced enforcement powers will increase scrutiny of NMW compliance in the care sector.

surveillanceStatus.note: ERA 2025 is the highest-priority surveillance target. Every commencement order, consultation response, and regulation is a Class 1 Alert requiring 24-hour CEO ratification under KLIA-001 Layer 1.
pendingAmendments: Over 25 consultations in progress or forthcoming
Over 80 sets of regulations required for full implementation
Commencement orders expected in waves: Feb 2026 (done), April 2026, October 2026, 2027
Compensation cap removal — subject to review before commencement
